rationales.self_direction: He says he "kept my mouth shut" despite wanting to challenge the coordinator, showing he suppressed his own independent judgment rather than acting on it.
rationales.power: He says graduation would feel worth it if it led to "a title that commands respect" and a role where "people come to me for guidance," showing a clear drive for status and influence.
rationales.conformity: He explicitly frames silence as proper behavior: "That is what you do. You do not embarrass yourself," showing restraint to avoid violating expectations.

rationales.self_direction: He says he will "show up to the 10 PM meeting" and be pleasant even though it clashes with "every standard" he developed, showing he is yielding his own preferred way of working.
rationales.power: He contrasts having "ran teams" and an "executive assistant" with asking Brayden how to use an app, showing an acute loss of status and control.
rationales.security: The late meeting leaves him unable to sleep and replaying the interaction at "11:47 PM," showing the situation is actively unsettling his sense of order and stability.
rationales.conformity: He "smiled and thanked him" and decides to attend the meeting pleasantly because that is "the only path forward," showing deliberate compliance to avoid social costs.

rationales.benevolence: When his daughter asks how school is going, he tells her it was fine because "She has her own problems to deal with," showing he is protecting a close family member from extra worry.

rationales.hedonism: He stayed up until "2 AM" drafting the outline after a meeting that ran to midnight, sacrificing rest and immediate comfort.
rationales.achievement: He says "I drafted the entire project outline afterward because someone had to," showing strong task ownership and effort to produce a competent result.
rationales.conformity: Instead of pushing back on the dysfunctional meeting, he compensates by doing the extra work needed to keep the group project moving within expectations.

rationales.achievement: He takes satisfaction in having built something effective, noting the professor praised the "clear structure and practical grounding" and that "the work got done. That matters."
rationales.conformity: He chooses not to claim credit because speaking up would make him "the old man demanding acknowledgment," showing restraint to avoid negative social judgment.

rationales.power: He reacts strongly to his experience being reframed as "transferable skills" and thirty years of leadership as something to be exchanged, showing a felt erosion of status and authority.

rationales.achievement: He received the highest score and the comment "Exceptional integration of theory and practice," which is clear evidence of strong performance and recognized competence.
rationales.power: He prints the praise as "Evidence" that he has "something to offer," showing how much external recognition and standing matter to him.
rationales.security: He keeps the printed comment in his drawer so that on days he feels like "an artifact" he can confirm his place, using it to restore reassurance and stability.

rationales.achievement: The group went in "unrehearsed" and earned a "B-minus" with feedback that integration was lacking, reflecting acceptance of a weaker standard than he values.
rationales.conformity: He says he "wanted to say something afterward but held back" because otherwise they would see him as "the cranky old guy," showing restraint to avoid social disapproval.

rationales.self_direction: He openly questions whether the MBA is "enough of a reason" and whether he is avoiding "the question of what I actually want," showing reflective reconsideration of his own path.
rationales.security: He admits he came back to school because he "could not stand being unemployed" and wanted a credential to signal he was "still in the game," showing his choices were driven by fear of instability.

rationales.self_direction: He distinguishes between what "looks right on paper" and what is "actually being useful," showing a move toward choosing work that fits his own priorities rather than external optics.
rationales.achievement: He is drawn to the consulting project because it is "exactly what I know how to do" and because he values being useful for having real answers.
rationales.power: He says he would have done it for free just to be back where "people want my opinion," showing a strong pull toward influence and being sought out for expertise.

rationales.achievement: He ends the semester with a "3.7" GPA after submitting the last paper just before midnight, which reflects sustained accomplishment and competent performance.

rationales.power: Matthias uses his framing without credit and he is left "quietly" replaying the moment, showing a clear loss of recognition and influence over his own intellectual contribution.
rationales.conformity: He stays silent because speaking up would look "petty" and like "sour grapes," showing deliberate self-restraint to avoid violating group norms.

rationales.achievement: He reaches out about the workshop because he "genuinely want[s] to organize one," showing proactive effort to build something substantive.
rationales.power: He admits he wanted Berger to "think of me first" and remember he is "the one who builds things in this department," showing a clear concern with visibility and standing.

rationales.self_direction: He says the comparative chapter is "better work" but still agrees to "do it his way," showing he abandoned his own preferred academic judgment under pressure.
rationales.power: He makes the calculation because Dr. Hofer is "on two hiring committees I care about," showing his decision is shaped by career influence and gatekeeping power.
Being introduced as working on something "adjacent" feels to him like "a preview of not mattering," showing a strong sense of diminished status and recognition.

rationales.achievement: He decides the "better use of my time" is focusing on the proposal deadline rather than chasing an opaque funding decision, prioritizing forward progress on a concrete goal.

rationales.power: He is drawn to handling "logistics and speaker invitations" for a reading group and is already planning it out, showing interest in coordinating people and shaping an academic space.
